1: 'After this Paul left Athens and went to Corinth' — the departure from Athens ends the Areopagus mission (Acts 17:16-34) and begins the eighteen-month Corinthian ministry. The transition marks a strategic shift: Corinth is a commercial crossroads, not an intellectual center; Paul's subsequent letters reveal that his Corinthian preaching deliberately avoided rhetorical display (1 Corinthians 2:1-4). The Athens-to-Corinth movement also corresponds to a shift from the philosophical register of Acts 17:22-31 to the cross-centered proclamation of 1 Corinthians 1:23.
2: 'A Jew named Aquila, a native of Pontus, who had recently come from Italy with his wife Priscilla, because Claudius had ordered all Jews to leave Rome' — the expulsion under Claudius (ca. 49-50 AD) is corroborated by Suetonius (Claudius 25.4 — 'Since the Jews constantly made disturbances at the instigation of Chrestus, he expelled them from Rome'). 'Chrestus' is likely 'Christus' — the dispute over the messianic claims of Jesus was splitting Roman synagogues. Aquila and Priscilla are likely already believers when Paul meets them; their synagogue dispute would explain their expulsion alongside other Jewish Christians.
3: 'Because he was a tentmaker as they were, he stayed and worked with them' — skēnopoios (tentmaker, leather-worker) is Paul's trade, consistent with his manual-labor principle (1 Thessalonians 2:9; 1 Corinthians 9:12-15; 2 Thessalonians 3:8). The philosophical tradition required teachers to receive fees; Paul's refusal to take support from Corinth (2 Corinthians 11:7-9) was a deliberate counter-convention: the teacher who works with his hands is not a sophist selling wisdom. The trade-based friendship with Aquila and Priscilla becomes one of the most enduring partnerships of Paul's ministry.
4: 'Every Sabbath he reasoned (dielēgeto — was dialoguing/disputing) in the synagogue, trying to persuade Jews and Greeks' — dielēgeto (imperfect = ongoing, repeated action) is the characteristic Lukan verb for Paul's synagogue method: not monologue proclamation but dialogical reasoning. 'Persuade' (epeithein — to persuade, imperfect = kept attempting) combined with dielēgeto creates the portrait of a reasoned, interactive theological engagement. 'Jews and Greeks' — the synagogue's Gentile adherents (sebomenoi — God-fearers) are included in Paul's audience from the beginning of the Corinthian synagogue phase.
5: 'When Silas and Timothy came from Macedonia, Paul devoted himself exclusively to preaching (syneicheto tō logō — was constrained/pressed by the word), testifying to the Jews that Jesus was the Messiah' — the textual tradition at syneicheto offers two readings: some MSS read tō logō (by the word) and others tō pneumati (by the Spirit, disp=3); the MKT follows 'Spirit' as primary. The arrival of Silas and Timothy (with financial support from Macedonian churches — 2 Corinthians 11:8-9) freed Paul from tentmaking, enabling full-time proclamation. 'Testifying that Jesus was the Messiah' — the content: the specific claim that Jesus of Nazareth is the Christ (ton Christon einai Iēsoun) is the central Jewish stumbling block.
6: 'When they opposed Paul and became abusive, he shook out his clothes in protest and said, Your blood be on your own heads!' — the symbolic action (ektinaxamenos ta himatia — shaking out the garments) echoes Nehemiah's curse-gesture (Nehemiah 5:13 — 'I shook out the fold of my garment') and is the escalated form of the dust-shaking from feet (Acts 13:51). 'Your blood be on your own heads' — the blood-guilt formula draws on Ezekiel 33:4-5: if the watchman warns and the people do not hear, 'his blood shall be on his own head.' Paul has fulfilled his prophetic-watchman duty; the guilt for rejection now belongs to the rejectors. 'I am innocent; from now on I will go to the Gentiles' — the Gentile-turn formula repeated from Acts 13:46.
7: 'Paul went to the house of Titius Justus, a worshipper of God (sebomenou ton theon — one fearing/revering God)' — the God-fearer designation (sebomenos) marks Titius Justus as a Gentile synagogue-adherent: he has adopted monotheism and Jewish ethics but is uncircumcised. His house is strategically located 'next door to the synagogue' — Paul's Gentile base of operations is literally adjacent to the Jewish community he has just left. The geographical irony (the synagogue's Gentile neighbors receive what the synagogue rejected) is characteristic of Luke's narrative theology.
8: 'Crispus, the synagogue leader (archisynagōgos), and his entire household believed in the Lord; and many of the Corinthians who heard Paul believed and were baptised' — Crispus's conversion is corroborated by 1 Corinthians 1:14 (Paul baptised Crispus himself, one of the very few he personally baptised). The synagogue leader's conversion is the highest-profile loss the Corinthian Jewish community sustained. 'His entire household believed' — the household-unit conversion pattern consistent throughout Acts. 'Many of the Corinthians' — the city's general population begins to respond beyond the synagogue community.
9: 'One night the Lord spoke to Paul in a vision: Do not be afraid; keep on speaking, do not be silent' — the Lord's vision (en horamati — in a vision) uses the standard prophetic-vision vocabulary (horama: Acts 10:3; Acts 16:9; Acts 18:9). The double negative command (mē phobou, mē siōpēsēs — do not fear, do not be silent) addresses two temptations that would naturally follow synagogue opposition: fear of violence and the temptation to withdraw. The night timing and visionary form (as opposed to waking auditory experience) is the standard divine-warning vehicle (Genesis 26:24; Acts 46:2-3; 1 Kings 3:5).
10: 'For I am with you, and no one is going to attack and harm you, because I have many people in this city' — 'I am with you' (egō eimi meta sou) is the divine-presence formula used at commissioning moments throughout the OT (Exodus 3:12; Joshua 1:5, 9; Jeremiah 1:8, 19) — the Emmanuel-formula. 'I have many people in this city' (laos estin moi polys en tē polei tautē) — the divine claim over Corinth before they have converted: God's elect in Corinth are already his people who have not yet heard. The vision explains why Paul must not be silent: there is a harvest already prepared.
11: 'So Paul stayed in Corinth for a year and a half, teaching them the word of God' (logon tou theou — the word of God; disp=3 for logos) — the eighteen months (eniayton kai mēnas hax) is the longest fixed-duration stay Luke records in the second missionary journey. 'The word of God' as logos is the gospel-proclamation content: the message that Paul teaches is identified as God's own word. The long stay corresponds to the Lord's vision-promise: God has people here, so Paul must preach long enough to find them all.
12: 'While Gallio was proconsul (anthypatos) of Achaia, the Jews of Corinth made a united attack on Paul and brought him to the place of judgment (bēma — tribunal/judgment seat)' — Gallio's proconsulate is dateable from the Delphi Inscription (ca. 51-52 AD), providing one of the firmest chronological anchors for Pauline chronology. The bēma (raised judicial platform) is the same word used for God's judgment seat (Romans 14:10; 2 Corinthians 5:10) — Luke's placement of Paul before an earthly bēma prefigures the cosmic bēma before which all will appear.
13: 'This man is persuading the people to worship God in ways contrary to the law (para ton nomon — contrary to the law)' — the charge is deliberately ambiguous: para ton nomon could refer to the Jewish Torah or Roman law. The Jewish accusers probably intend the Torah (Paul's messianic preaching disrupts Jewish worship patterns); Gallio will interpret the charge as referring to internal Jewish disputes (Acts 18:15). The nomos (disp=3) here is the Jewish Law — the word that governs the covenant community's religious practice.
14: 'If you Jews were making a complaint about some misdemeanour or serious crime (adikēma ti ē rhadiourgēma ponēron), it would be reasonable for me to listen to you' — Gallio distinguishes between actionable legal offenses (adikēma: a wrong done; rhadiourgēma ponēron: a vicious/serious villainy) and matters of Jewish internal dispute. His willingness to hear genuine criminal complaints (Acts 18:14) vs. his refusal to adjudicate religious questions (Acts 18:15) establishes the legal precedent that Paul's movement does not constitute a Roman criminal offense.
15: 'But since it involves questions about words and names and your own law (tou nomou hymōn — your own law), settle the matter yourselves. I will not be a judge of such things' — Gallio identifies the dispute as involving logos (disp=3 — 'words': the meaning of terms), onomata (names: the identity-claim about Jesus as Messiah), and nomos (disp=3 — 'your law': internal Jewish legal interpretation). All three belong to Jewish internal discourse; Roman magistrates have no jurisdiction over theological disputes. The Gallio ruling implicitly classifies Christianity as an intra-Jewish matter, providing de facto legal protection for the early mission.
16: 'So he drove them off (apēlasen autous — dismissed/drove them away)' — Gallio's dismissal (apēlasen, aorist of apelaunō — to drive/push away) is abrupt and decisive. The proconsul exercises his authority to clear his tribunal of cases that do not belong to it. The brevity of the verse mirrors the brevity of Gallio's engagement: he considers the matter not worth his attention, and says so by action rather than extended argument.
17: 'Then the crowd seized Sosthenes the synagogue leader and beat him in front of the proconsul; and Gallio showed no concern whatever' — the beating of Sosthenes (etypton — kept striking, imperfect) by 'the crowd' is ambiguous: some MSS specify 'the Greeks,' suggesting a spontaneous anti-Jewish action by the Corinthian populace seizing on Gallio's dismissal as permission for mob violence. Gallio's indifference (ouden toutōn tō Galliōni emelen — none of these things concerned Gallio) is the Roman administrator's calculated non-intervention in non-Roman affairs. Sosthenes may be the same person addressed in 1 Corinthians 1:1 as co-sender — perhaps his persecution became the occasion of his conversion.
18: 'Paul stayed on in Corinth for some time. Then he left the brothers and sisters and sailed for Syria, accompanied by Priscilla and Aquila. Before he sailed, he had his hair cut off at Cenchreae because of a vow he had made' — the hair-cutting (keiramenos tēn kephalēn) at Cenchreae (Corinth's eastern port) is a Nazirite vow-completion gesture (Numbers 6:18 — the Nazirite shaves his head at the vow's end). Paul observes Torah practices alongside his proclamation of freedom from the Law — the consistency with the Jerusalem council's decision (Acts 15:19-21) and his own principle of becoming 'all things to all people' (1 Corinthians 9:20-22). Priscilla and Aquila's inclusion in the travel party marks them as full partners in the mission.
19: 'They arrived at Ephesus, where Paul left Priscilla and Aquila. He himself went into the synagogue and reasoned with the Jews' — the Ephesus stop is brief (Acts 18:20-21 — he declines to stay longer despite their invitation) but strategically significant: Paul establishes the initial synagogue contact (dielexato — he dialogued/reasoned) and leaves Priscilla and Aquila as his representatives. Their presence in Ephesus will be decisive for Apollos's correction (Acts 18:26). Paul's synagogue entry before his departure plants the seed that his extended return will cultivate (Acts 19:1-10).
20: 'When they asked him to spend more time with them, he declined' — the Ephesians' invitation (erōtōntōn autōn — they were asking/requesting, imperfect = continued asking) and Paul's refusal (ouk epeneusen — he did not nod assent) creates a reversal of the Macedonian vision pattern (Acts 16:9 — 'come over and help us'): here the Gentiles plead for more time and Paul says no. His haste to return to Jerusalem and Antioch (Acts 18:21-22) is the controlling factor. The Ephesian receptivity makes the brevity of this first visit rhetorically effective — their longing for more sets up the extended ministry of Acts 19:1-10.
21: 'But as he left, he promised, I will come back if it is God's will (tou theou thelontos — if God wills)' — the conditional 'if God wills' (the deo voluntate formula) is Paul's standard acknowledgment that itinerary is divinely governed (Romans 1:10; Acts 15:32; 1 Corinthians 4:19; Acts 16:7; James 4:13-15). The promise is not merely polite; it is the expression of the missionary's dependence on divine direction. The promise is fulfilled in Acts 19:1 — Paul returns to Ephesus via the interior route.
22: 'When he landed at Caesarea, he went up to Jerusalem and greeted the church and then went down to Antioch' — 'went up to Jerusalem' (anabas) and 'went down to Antioch' (katebē) use the standard Palestinian topographical verbs: Jerusalem is always anabainō (go up) regardless of compass direction — it is elevated both geographically and in religious significance. The 'greeting the church' (aspásamenos tēn ekklēsian) is a rapid visit, not an extended stay; Paul's third missionary journey begins immediately (Acts 18:23).
23: 'After spending some time in Antioch, Paul set out from there and travelled from place to place throughout the region of Galatia and Phrygia, strengthening all the disciples' — the 'region of Galatia and Phrygia' (tēn Galatikēn chōran kai Phrygian — Galatian region and Phrygian region) is the interior route that bypasses the coast before arriving at Ephesus (Acts 19:1). 'Strengthening (epistērizōn — making stable/firm) all the disciples' — the participle establishes the purpose of the journey: not evangelism but consolidation and follow-up of existing communities planted in the second journey.
24: 'A Jew named Apollos, a native of Alexandria, came to Ephesus. He was a learned man (anēr logios — a word-skilled/eloquent man), with a thorough knowledge (dynastatos — powerful) of the Scriptures' — Alexandria was the intellectual capital of the diaspora, home of the LXX translation and the Jewish philosophical tradition (Philo). Logios (word-skilled, eloquent) is the highest compliment for a rhetorical culture: Apollos is both eloquent in speech and learned in content. 'Thorough knowledge (dynastatos — capable/powerful) in the Scriptures' — his power is textual competence; he can demonstrate from the Hebrew Bible. 1 Corinthians 1:12 and Acts 3:4-6 show that Apollos became a significant figure at Corinth.
25: 'He had been instructed in the way of the Lord, and he spoke with great fervour (zeōn tō pneumati — boiling/fervent in spirit; disp=3 for pneuma) and taught about Jesus accurately, though he knew only the baptism of John' — zeōn tō pneumati (boiling/fervent in spirit) — pneuma here may refer to Apollos's own spirit (human fervor) rather than the Holy Spirit; the MKT 'great fervour' preserves the ambiguity. The irony: Apollos teaches accurately about Jesus (akribōs — precisely, carefully) but knows only John's preparatory baptism, not the post-resurrection baptism in Jesus's name. His accurate-but-incomplete knowledge is the occasion for Priscilla and Aquila's correction.
26: 'He began to speak boldly in the synagogue. When Priscilla and Aquila heard him, they invited him to their home and explained to him the way of God (tēn tou theou hodon — the way of God; disp=2 for theos) more adequately (akribesteron — more precisely/accurately)' — the correction of Apollos by Priscilla and Aquila is notable: Priscilla is named first (Acts 18:2, 18, 26 — the majority of references put her before Aquila), suggesting she was the more prominent teacher. The private setting ('invited him to their home') and the term akribesteron (more accurately — comparative of the same akribōs used for Apollos's teaching in Acts 18:25) show that the correction is gentle and precise: not a rebuke but an extension of what Apollos already knows.
27: 'When Apollos wanted to go to Achaia, the brothers and sisters encouraged him and wrote to the disciples there to welcome him. When he arrived, he was a great help to those who by grace (dia tēs charitos — through grace; disp=2 for charis) had believed' — charis (grace) here denotes the divine enabling-favor that produced the Achaian believers' faith: they believed 'through grace' — not by their own capacity but by God's gift (Ephesians 2:8-9). Apollos's role is strengthening those already converted by Paul's mission: he waters what Paul planted (1 Corinthians 3:6). The letter of recommendation (the 'brothers and sisters encouraged him and wrote') is the earliest instance of a NT letter of commendation (cf. 2 Corinthians 3:1-2).
28: 'He vigorously refuted (diakatelenchteto — thoroughly confuted/convicted) his Jewish opponents in public debate, proving from the Scriptures that Jesus was the Messiah (ton Christon einai Iēsoun)' — diakatelenchteto (imperfect = continued/repeated refutation) is a compound intensive: he was thoroughly and continuously defeating his opponents in argument. 'Proving from the Scriptures' (epideiknys dia tōn graphōn — showing through the writings) is the standard apostolic demonstration-method: the case for Jesus's messiahship is made from the Hebrew Bible itself, not from apostolic authority alone. Apollos's Alexandrian education makes him the most scripture-literate debater the early mission produced.
